title: The unlocked door
prompt: On a hardened box, no file should be world-writable — writable by ANYONE. One file in this tree is. find can hunt by permission: find . -perm -o+w -type f finds files where 'others' have write access. The offender holds the flag.

details.restate: Exactly one file in the tree is writable by everyone (the dreaded 777). Find it by PERMISSION — not by reading files one by one — and the flag is in its comments.

io.because: -perm -o+w matches files where 'o' (others, i.e. anyone) can write
three w's across the row is the visual signature of 777

terms.meaning: any user on the system can modify the file — on real servers, an open door

help.plain: find filters on permissions the same way it filters on names.
help.analogy: Walking a hotel corridor at night pushing every door — you're looking for the one that swings open.
help.mnemonic: -perm -o+w: o for Others, w for Write — the door anyone can open.
help.nudge: You've used find with -name and -size. What's the flag for matching permissions?
